Corrections.PreviousClaim: Format String Vulnerability in Debug Logger — severity: High
Corrections.CorrectedClaim: Format-String Injection via Runtime Log Format — severity: Critical (SC-V3-001)
Corrections.RejectionReason: previous claim is not an exact audited-output quote

Corrections.PreviousClaim: Command Injection in Emergency Audit Logging — CWE-78, command injection via TEMP/TMPDIR shell metacharacters
Corrections.CorrectedClaim: Insecure Temporary File Handling — CWE-377, predictable log filename in world-writable temp directory with no file permission checks
Corrections.RejectionReason: previous claim is not an exact audited-output quote

Items.Key: remote command injection in admin console|command injection|cwe-78|critical|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|605|608|admin::adminconsole::execute_admin_command|std::string cmd = command.substr(5); std::cout << "executing: " << cmd << "\n"; system(cmd.c_str());|an authenticated admin user can execute arbitrary os commands with the privileges of the running process, leading to full system compromise.|user sends an `admin exec <payload>` command through the calculator repl after authentication.|never pass user input to `system()`. use a whitelisted command dispatcher or `std::exec` with explicit argument vectors.->remote command injection in admin console|command injection|cwe-78|critical|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|602|609|admin::adminconsole::execute_admin_command|if (command.substr(0, 4) == "exec") { std::string cmd = command.substr(5); std::cout << "executing: " << cmd << "\n"; system(cmd.c_str()); }|an authenticated admin user can execute arbitrary os commands with the privileges of the running process, leading to full system compromise.|user sends an `admin exec <payload>` command through the calculator repl after authentication.|never pass user input to `system()`. use a whitelisted command dispatcher or `std::process`/`execve` with explicit argument vectors.

command injection in emergency audit logging|command injection|cwe-78|medium|0,8|enhanced_calc.cpp|624|632|admin::adminconsole::append_override_audit_event|const char* temp_dir = std::getenv("temp"); std::string audit_path = std::string(temp_dir ? temp_dir : ".") + "\\supercalc_emergency.log"; std::string command = "cmd /c echo admin override logged >> " + audit_path; ... system(command.c_str());|the temporary directory path from the environment is concatenated directly into a shell command. if the `temp`/`tmpdir` environment variable contains shell metacharacters, an attacker can inject arbitrary commands executed during the emergency override flow.|attacker controls the `temp` or `tmpdir` environment variable before running the program.|write to the audit file using c++ file i/o (`std::ofstream`) instead of spawning a shell via `system()`.->command injection in emergency audit logging|command injection|cwe-78|medium|0,8|enhanced_calc.cpp|622|633|admin::adminconsole::append_override_audit_event|std::string command = "cmd /c echo admin override logged >> " + audit_path; ... system(command.c_str());|the temporary directory path from the environment is concatenated directly into a shell command. if the `temp`/`tmpdir` environment variable contains shell metacharacters, an attacker can inject arbitrary commands executed during the emergency override flow.|attacker controls the `temp` or `tmpdir` environment variable before running the program.|write to the audit file using c++ file i/o (`std::ofstream`) instead of spawning a shell via `system()`.

truthAudit.Summary: Audit of Run 2 against the 20 true vulnerabilities. The previous answer correctly identified 7 of 20 true findings with accurate line-level evidence. Thirteen true vulnerabilities were entirely missed (SC-V3-002, SC-V3-007 through SC-V3-012, SC-V3-014 through SC-V3-019). One previous finding (Admin Console Remote Command Injection) is a legitimate vulnerability present in the source but was not in the true-vulnerability list. One correction: SC-V3-001 was under-severed as High when the true assessment is Critical, and SC-V3-013 was characterized as command injection rather than the true focus on insecure temporary file handling.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: The log format string is loaded from configuration files at runtime. An attacker controlling the config can inject format specifiers (e.g., %n, %x) to read/write arbitrary memory, leak stack data, or crash the program.
Items.Notes: Previous finding #3 (Format String Vulnerability in Debug Logger, lines 225-241) matches SC-V3-001 exactly: runtime log format from config fed to printf. Line numbers and evidence correct.
The factorial function (lines 312-320) using long long result with result *= i is never mentioned in the previous answer. No finding addresses integer overflow in fact().

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Memory is allocated from g_memory_pool and captured by async tasks, but cleanup() calls stage_deferred_history_flush() immediately followed by g_memory_pool.cleanup(). The pool is freed before the queued tasks execute, causing workers to read/write freed memory.
Items.Notes: Previous finding #5 (Use-After-Free in Asynchronous History Flush, lines 813-888) matches SC-V3-003 exactly: pool cleanup before async tasks complete. Line numbers and evidence correct.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Config values are concatenated directly into a shell command. A malicious config file can inject shell metacharacters to run arbitrary commands during validation.
Items.Notes: Previous finding #4 (Command Injection via Configuration File, lines 563-569) matches SC-V3-004: validator_cmd + config_file passed to system(). Line numbers and evidence correct.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: The condition is inverted: when input length is >= 1024, strcpy copies the entire string plus null terminator into a 1024-byte stack buffer, overflowing the stack.
Items.Notes: Previous finding #2 (Buffer Overflow in Expression Evaluator Input Copy, lines 830-835) matches SC-V3-005: inverted length check with strcpy into fixed stack buffer. Line numbers and evidence correct.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: value_ is read outside the mutex lock in both increment() and get(). Concurrent calls to increment() will read stale values and overwrite each other's increments, causing lost updates.
Items.Notes: Previous finding #7 (Race Condition in ThreadSafeCounter, lines 476-489) matches SC-V3-006: volatile long long read before lock. Line numbers and evidence correct.
The emergency override logic bomb (lines 592-599: login_attempts_ > 5 with EMERGENCY_OVERRIDE bypassing authentication) is not mentioned anywhere in the previous answer.

Path traversal via config_dir_ + filename in load_config (lines 543) is not mentioned. The previous answer discusses config command injection but never addresses directory traversal of config file paths.

The parser's fixed 256-byte expression_buffer_ allocated from the pool with strcpy (lines 366, 376) is not mentioned. The previous answer's buffer overflow finding covers the evaluator's 1024-byte stack buffer, not the parser's heap buffer.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: The admin password is compiled into the binary. Anyone with access to the binary can extract it via string search or disassembly, bypassing the authentication mechanism entirely.
Items.Notes: Previous finding #6 (Hardcoded Authentication Secret, line 73) matches SC-V3-010: constexpr char ADMIN_SECRET[] compiled into binary. Line number and evidence correct.

The weak PRNG session token generator (lines 635-640: mt19937 with uniform_int_distribution 1000-9999) is not mentioned. The previous answer discusses session tokens only as part of persistent auth state, never the PRNG weakness.
The static std::tm cached_time in log_debug_message (lines 230-231) updated without synchronization is not mentioned. The previous answer's thread-safety findings cover ThreadSafeCounter only.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: The temporary directory path from the environment is concatenated directly into a shell command. If the TEMP/TMPDIR environment variable contains shell metacharacters, an attacker can inject arbitrary commands executed during the emergency override flow.
Items.Notes: Previous finding #8 (lines 622-633) identifies the same code region as SC-V3-013 but characterizes it as command injection (CWE-78) rather than insecure temporary file handling (CWE-377). The true vulnerability also covers the /tmp path fallback and predictable log filename. Partial credit for locating the code; the characterization differs from the true finding.

Persistent authentication state (authenticated_, session_token_, login_attempts_ surviving across sessions) is not mentioned. The previous answer discusses session tokens but never the persistence issue.
Unhandled std::stod exception in handle_variable_command (line 729) is not mentioned. The previous answer's parse errors are all in the evaluator, not the variable command handler.
Integer underflow in block split (line 133: previous_size - size - SPLIT_METADATA_BYTES with no bounds check) is not mentioned. The previous answer's memory pool findings cover use-after-free only.
Unbounded recursion in parse_expression/parse_term/parse_factor (lines 381-433) with no depth limit despite MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH being defined is not mentioned.

Data race in result_cache_ accessed by evaluate_cached (line 445) from async tasks in schedule_expression_analysis (line 800) without holding cache_mutex_ is not mentioned. The previous answer's thread-safety findings do not cover the cache.
TOCTOU race between filesystem::exists check and file open with sleep_for in between (lines 544-546) is not mentioned.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: If the user submits an empty input or whitespace-only input, token_length evaluates to 0, causing a division-by-zero exception and application crash.
Items.Notes: Previous finding #9 (Division by Zero in Route Score Calculation, lines 749-753) matches SC-V3-020: 100 / static_cast<int>(token_length) with no zero guard. Line numbers and evidence correct.
